artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching up – trucks, kids, and Mom

Hey Eric,

Finally sitting down with a cup of coffee after digging out the end of the driveway (again). Figured I’d give you the longer version of what’s been going on up here.

Work first: I’ve got the “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Street Maintenance” back on the February 2, 2026 council agenda. Same basic deal I told you about over Christmas – replacing Unit #24, the 2006 International 7600 with 178,000 miles and over 10,000 engine hours. Quote from Nuss for the 2026 Freightliner 114SD came in at $298,450 under the State CPV contract, which is actually a hair under the $300k we had in the 2026–2030 CIP. Money’s all coming out of the Street Equipment Replacement Fund, so at least I don’t have to argue about levies.

December’s storm is still hanging over me. When the hydraulic pump on #24 failed and the truck sat dead for almost two days, we had to shell out $3,750 for a private hauler to keep the northeast quadrant open. That was the same week our furnace died at the house on Willow and cost us $2,900, and I was still trying to help you with those two months of mortgage after the mill layoff in November. It felt like every dollar was going out the door.

On top of that, I’m feeling my age. After that Jan 8–9, 2025 blizzard where I logged 31.5 hours straight at the East River Road garage, Dr. Steven Larson at Cuyuna read me the riot act on February 3 about blood pressure and heart attack risk. I’m trying not to be the guy sleeping on a cot at the shop every storm.

How are things in St. Cloud? Any bites on more contracts for your crew on Division Street? We’re heading to Evan’s hockey at Essentia Sports Center Tuesday if you feel like driving up.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Catching up – trucks, kids, and Mom

artifacts.content:
Subject: Mom, storms, and this new truck

Hi Mom,

Just wanted to check in after dropping you back at Baxter Senior Villas yesterday. I know the Tuesday 7:30 a.m. cardiac rehab runs to Essentia’s Heart & Vascular Center in Brainerd take a lot out of you, especially since that mild stroke back in October 2023, and I hate that I’m sometimes dragging in straight from the Public Works shop on no sleep.

You remember that January 4, 2025 storm – I was on from 7 p.m. to 6:30 a.m. babysitting cranky old Unit #24 through another round of electrical faults, then drove straight from the Sylvester/East River Road garage to your neurology follow‑up with Dr. Patel at St. Joe’s. I was so wiped I nodded off while he was explaining your blood thinner change. That scared me more than I let on.

That’s a big reason I’m pushing the council to move on the replacement truck on February 2. The summary I sent them focuses on the December 2025 hydraulic failure on #24 (the one that sidelined us for two days and forced the $3,750 private hauler in the northeast quadrant), but the personal side is that every time that truck dies, I end up picking up extra overnight shifts. My back and shoulders are already barking, and Dr. Carlson at Brainerd Orthopaedics has me in PT three times a week at about $120 a pop.

The good news is the new 2026 Freightliner is fully funded in the Street Equipment Replacement Fund and comes in just under the $300,000 we planned in the Capital Improvement Plan, so it shouldn’t affect anyone’s taxes.

Evan’s got hockey at Essentia Health Sports Center Tuesday and Maddie’s band concert is Wednesday at Forestview. If you’re up for it and the weather cooperates, I can swing by Baxter and bring you to one of them.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Mom, storms, and this new truck

artifacts.content:
Subject: Council timing vs. cardiology

Laura,

Can you be home by 7 on March 3? I’m trying to keep my council presentation that night to 10 minutes with 5 minutes for questions – about 15 minutes total – so I can get over to Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic for that 7:30 p.m. cardiology follow‑up with Dr. Karen Lofgren. She still wants to talk through the abnormal stress test from January last year and how that Jan 8–9, 2025 blizzard (31.5 hours straight at the East River Road garage) didn’t exactly help my numbers.

HR also asked me to keep things tight because they want enough time afterward for the closed session on my February 2025 performance improvement plan – the one tied to the January 30, 2025 written warning about snow‑removal complaints. Not my favorite topic. Between that and Karen Olson’s counseling last March after Councilmember David Larson forwarded those plow complaints near Brainerd High and our own block on Willow, I feel like I’m living under a microscope.

Add in Evan’s mess – the underage drinking citation at the Brainerd High homecoming game last fall and the 7:00 p.m. counseling sessions at Northern Pines Mental Health Center – and it feels like every week is scheduled in 15‑minute chunks. At least Maddie’s biggest drama right now is her clarinet solo.

On the bright side, the truck item itself is straightforward: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Street Maintenance,” 2026 Freightliner 114SD, $298,450 from Nuss under the CPV contract, all budgeted in the Street Equipment Replacement Fund and the 2026–2030 CIP. If I stick to the talking points, we should be in and out.

Let me know if you can swap your Essentia shifts that night so one of us is always with the kids.

– M

Title: Truck replacement – personal notes

I need this truck ordered in February 2026 so we hit that 10–12 month lead time and have it ready for the 2026–27 winter. The official story is all in the CIP and the agenda memo: Unit #24 (2006 International 7600, ~178,000 miles, 10,000+ engine hours) is at the end of its life. December 2025 hydraulic pump failure sidelined it for almost two days, forced us to bring in a private hauler for $3,750, staff fatigue, resident complaints from the northeast quadrant, etc. Replacement is a 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem‑axle with stainless dump, underbody scraper, front plow, combo liquid/salt spreader, $298,450 from Nuss via State CPV, fully funded in the Street Equipment Replacement Fund and under the $300,000 in the 2026–2030 CIP.

The personal side I keep out of the packet:

– My own hospitalization for stress‑related chest pain at Essentia–St. Joseph’s in February 2024 and the six‑week staffing hole that left in Street and Parks. We leaned on overtime and that junk truck, and it about broke me.

– Mom’s health: mild stroke October 2023, now at Baxter Senior Villas, weekly 7:30 a.m. cardiac rehab and neurology follow‑ups at St. Joe’s. I’ve already had one morning (Jan 4, 2025) where I drove her on zero sleep after an all‑night shift babysitting #24’s electrical faults.

– Evan and Maddie: his underage drinking citation at the Brainerd High homecoming game and mandated counseling at Northern Pines; her asthma hospitalization at Essentia in January 2024 that helped drive up the City’s health premiums and triggered Karen Olson’s talk about cutting $150,000 from my overtime line.

Bottom line: a reliable plow isn’t just about lane‑miles; it’s about not running myself and the crew into the ground.

Title: Council – Freightliner 114SD truck purchase

Time: Monday, February 2, 2026, 6:15–6:30 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Council, City Administrator Karen Olson, Finance Director, Michael Hanson

Description:

Agenda item: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Street Maintenance.” Target 15 minutes total – 10‑minute staff presentation, 5 minutes for Council questions and motion.

Talking points to hit:

• Need to replace Unit #24, 2006 International 7600 (≈178,000 miles, 10,000+ engine hours).
• December 2025 storm: hydraulic pump failure, truck out nearly two full days; had to contract private hauler for $3,750 to keep northeast quadrant and school/bus routes open.
• Prior incidents to reference generally (no names): Jan 10–11, 2025 Evergreen Street minor crash after ABS warning light; Feb 6, 2024 spreader freeze on College Drive/Oaklawn that led to resident confrontation and HR de‑escalation training.
• Proposed replacement: 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem‑axle, stainless dump body, underbody scraper, front plow, combination liquid/salt spreader. Purchase through State of Minnesota CPV contract from Nuss Truck & Equipment.
• Total cost with tax and estimated upfitting: $298,450.00, fully budgeted in the 2026 Street Equipment Replacement Fund and consistent with the 2026–2030 Capital Improvement Plan (planning estimate $300,000).
• Benefits: improved reliability, reduced downtime and overtime, updated lighting/camera/ergonomics to cut back strain on operators’ backs and shoulders, more precise salt application to reduce usage and environmental impact on the Mississippi and area lakes.

Personal reminder: wrap on time so I can get home to Willow Street, check in on Maddie before bed, and be rested enough for Mom’s Tuesday 7:30 a.m. cardiac rehab ride from Baxter to Essentia.

Subject: Budget talk and why I’m done limping along with #24

Laura,

Rough couple of days. Karen Olson had me in her office again going over the 2026 projections. She pulled out that January 8, 2025 draft where she showed a possible $150,000 reduction in my overtime line to offset the health insurance hit from a few big claims – including Maddie’s January 2024 asthma hospitalization at Essentia–St. Joe’s. It’s hard not to feel guilty that our own kid’s stay is part of the spreadsheet.

At the same time, she wants us to maintain the 8–10 hour plow‑out window for 120+ lane‑miles with a fleet that includes a 20‑year‑old lemon. I told her again that the “continue to operate Unit #24 with reactive repairs only” option is a fantasy. We’re already averaging $9,000–$11,000 a year in repairs on that thing. During the December 2025 storm it failed so badly we had to bring in that $3,750 private hauler and juggle routes, which meant Dave Miller pulled another 18‑hour shift. His wife is pressuring him to retire after the Brainerd Medical Center doc added a second blood pressure med in November 2023.

I’m baking all of that into my own thinking, even if the public memo just talks about reliability, safety features, and salt‑usage reductions. The Freightliner quote from Nuss is $298,450, which slots under the $300k we put in the 2026–2030 CIP, and the Street Equipment Replacement Fund has enough unencumbered balance. No levy increase, no bonding, no special assessment.

Meanwhile, we still have Evan’s mandated counseling sessions at Northern Pines from the underage drinking citation, my follow‑ups with Dr. Lofgren about that abnormal stress test, and Mom’s rides from Baxter Senior Villas to cardiac rehab. I’m tired of living in crisis mode every time it snows.

I’ll be late tonight – de‑icing briefing at the Sylvester shop – but save me some of your baked chicken if you can.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Budget talk and why I’m done limping along with #24

artifacts.content:
Thread with Laura – Dec 22, 2025

Mike: Long night. I was out plowing until almost 3:30 a.m. again.

Mike: Unit 24’s hydraulics crapped out on Oak Street by Brainerd High School around 1:45. Had to limp it back toward the yard and then call you from my city cell to check on Maddie.

Mike: She okay now? You said she’d seen those posts about spin‑outs on 371 and was having a full‑on panic attack at the house in Baxter.

Laura: She finally settled after we did the breathing thing her therapist at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic showed her. I still have to take her back there on Jan 5 for a follow‑up.

Mike: This is exactly why I’m pushing so hard to get the replacement truck on the Feb 2 council agenda. When the route through Willow Street and the school zone gets delayed because #24 dies, it’s not just angry residents – it’s our own kid melting down.

Laura: I get it. Just don’t burn yourself out. You’ve still got that March 3, 2025 7:30 p.m. cardiology follow‑up with Dr. Lofgren hanging over you, and Dr. Larson at Cuyuna already warned you after the Jan 8–9, 2025 blizzard that another 31.5‑hour stretch could push you toward a heart attack.

Mike: I know. That’s why I’m going to keep the “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Street Maintenance” item to 15 minutes – 10 to present, 5 for questions – and then get home.

Laura: Don’t forget Evan’s Northern Pines counseling at 7:00 p.m. on Feb 12 from the homecoming drinking mess, and Mom’s Tuesday 7:30 a.m. cardiac rehab runs from Baxter Senior Villas to Essentia. We’re juggling a lot.

Mike: Tell me about it. At least Moose just wants breakfast and a walk.
